step.title: Step 36
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 106, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 105 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 3.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing UP 10 times:
- Left feet: rows 39-43 -> rows 14-18 (moved up 25 rows!)
- Right feet: rows 39-43 -> rows 39-43 (didn't move!)
Wait, that's strange. The left feet moved up 25 rows (from 39-43 to 14-18), but the right feet didn't move at all. This suggests that the feet move independently, and the left feet moved up while the right feet were blocked or didn't move.
Actually, looking more carefully, the left feet moved from rows 39-43 to rows 14-18, which is the neck area. The right feet stayed at rows 39-43, which is the torso area.
This is very different from what I expected. I thought UP moves both feet up by the same amount. But it seems like the feet move independently, and the left feet moved up 25 rows while the right feet didn't move.
Wait, but earlier when I pressed UP, both feet moved up. Let me re-examine the earlier behavior.
Actually, I think the feet move independently, and they can move to different rows. The left feet are now at rows 14-18 (neck area), and the right feet are at rows 39-43 (torso area).
After pressing SPACE, the feet stayed at the same positions. So SPACE didn't move the feet this time.
Let me think about what SPACE does. Earlier, SPACE moved the feet from rows 29-33 to rows 39-43 (down 10 rows). But now, with the feet at rows 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43 (right), SPACE didn't move them.
Maybe SPACE only moves the feet when they're at the same row. Or maybe SPACE moves the feet to a specific target position.
Actually, looking at the image, the left feet are now at rows 14-18, cols 14-18, which is the neck area. The right feet are at rows 39-43, cols 44-48, which is the torso area.
I think I need to reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal is to have the feet at specific positions, and SPACE is used to "lock" or "complete" the level once the feet are in the correct positions.
Let me look at the initial state again:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet started at rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (arm area, center). The right feet started at rows 54-58, cols 44-48 (bottom of legs).
Maybe the goal is to swap the positions of the left and right feet:
- Left feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48 (bottom of legs)
- Right feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (arm area, center)
But this seems unlikely, as the feet are constrained to their respective sides of the body.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to have the feet at the "hands" at rows 29-38, cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right). But I can't get the right feet to cols 49-53 without moving the left feet from cols 14-18 to cols 9-13.
Actually, wait. The left feet are now at rows 14-18, cols 14-18. The right feet are at rows 39-43, cols 44-48. Maybe I need to move the left feet down to rows 29-38 and the right feet up to rows 29-38, and then press SPACE.
Let me try moving the left feet down and the right feet up to the same row.
Current feet:
- Left: rows 14-18, cols 14-18
- Right: rows 39-43, cols 44-48
To move the left feet down to rows 29-38: press DOWN 10-15 times.
To move the right feet up to rows 29-38: press UP 4-14 times.
But UP and DOWN affect both feet. If I press DOWN, both feet move down. If I press UP, both feet move up.
Wait, but earlier when I pressed UP 10 times, the left feet moved up 25 rows and the right feet didn't move. This suggests that the feet move independently, and the amount they move depends on their current position or the body shape.
Let me try pressing DOWN to move the left feet down from rows 14-18.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right)
# The feet seem to move independently. Let me test DOWN.

action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18
Feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
